title: The Habit, Not The Goal
description: Daily micro-practice beats weekly deep sessions. The tools work — but only if you use them.

blocks.content: Six modules. Thirty lessons. More tools than you can fit in a toolbox. You probably feel two things: "I get the framework" and "There's no way I'll remember all this." The second feeling is more honest. And it's fine. You don't need to remember all of it. You need to pick up the habit. Not the goal. The habit.
By the end of this lesson, you will have a Daily 5 routine and commit to starting tonight.

blocks.question: Match each of the 4 C's to the module that builds it:
blocks.explanation: Exactly. Control → M1 (Gap Check, 6-2-7). Confidence → M2 (Evidence File). Challenge → M3 (If-Then Plans). Commitment → M4 (Motivation Check).

blocks.content: Daily micro-practice beats weekly deep sessions. Five minutes daily beats 35 minutes weekly. The tools work — but only if you use them.

steps.label: Stage 1: Unconscious Incompetence
steps.content: You don't know what you don't know. Before this course, you didn't know about the chimp and the coach, the central governor, attentional flexibility. Operating without an owner's manual.

steps.label: Stage 2: Conscious Incompetence
steps.content: You know what you should be doing, but you're bad at it. The 6-2-7 feels awkward. You forget performance statements in races. You SEE the errors — which makes it feel like you're getting WORSE. This is the danger zone where most people quit.

steps.label: Stage 3: Conscious Competence
steps.content: You can do it, but it requires effort. You use the 6-2-7 on the start line and it works, but you have to deliberately remember. You catch the chimp mid-spiral, but it takes real energy. It works. It's just not automatic yet.

steps.label: Stage 4: Unconscious Competence
steps.content: It's automatic. You breathe through a race crisis without deciding to. Performance statements fire on their own. You're not DOING mental skills — you ARE mentally skilled. The tools are part of the operating system.

blocks.content: Stage 2 feels like getting worse. That's awareness outpacing skill — you can see the errors now, which is the prerequisite for fixing them. Expect it. Push through it.

items.title: 5 Minutes Daily > 30 Minutes Weekly
items.content: Ericsson's deliberate practice research (1993): expert performance requires spaced, focused practice. Short, frequent, intentional sessions are the fastest path from "I know this" to "I do this automatically." The brain consolidates skills through spacing, not cramming.

items.title: Habit Formation Research
items.content: Lally et al. (2010, European Journal of Social Psychology): habit formation takes 18-254 days, median 66. Exercise behaviors take 1.5x longer to automatize than eating or drinking habits. Mental habits should be easier — but only if you start. And only if you keep going past the point where it stops being novel.

items.title: The Competence Valley
items.content: The MAC approach (Gardner & Moore, 2004) confirmed it: daily mindfulness and acceptance practice, maintained as a habit, produced performance gains. Not occasional practice. Not pre-race-only. Daily, over time. Without the habit, skills decay. With the habit, skills compound.

items.label: Minute 1: Breathe (6-2-7)
items.detail: Four cycles. Your physiological reset. Activates the parasympathetic brake, anchors you in the present, creates the calm foundation for everything that follows.

items.label: Minute 2: Highlight Reel
items.detail: One vivid scene — empowering past moment or crucial future moment. Ten seconds of full sensory detail, first person, full immersion. You're priming the pump for confidence and self-efficacy.

items.label: Minute 3: Performance Statements
items.detail: Say your three statements — technical, pain response, process — silently or out loud. Repeat each several times. This is self-talk programming. Loading the ammunition before the chimp shows up.

items.label: Minute 4: Schema Statement
items.detail: Who you are. What you want. The strength and the aspiration. Don't just recite it — mean it. Identity maintenance that keeps the "I am" statement fresh and active.

items.label: Minute 5: Gratitude + Reflection
items.detail: Name one specific thing you're grateful for as an athlete today. Then one question: What's one thing I'm going to do well today? Grounds you in the present. Sends you into the day with direction.

blocks.content: Start tonight. Not next Monday. Tonight. Five minutes before bed. By day three, you'll have it memorized. By day seven, you stop reading the cards. The statements are yours.
I will start The Daily 5 tonight at [time].
The tools in this course work. But only if you use them.

Six modules. You've got the science, the tools, the framework. You'll notice the difference the first time you hit a wall and reach for a tool instead of an excuse. Five minutes a day. Not a goal — a habit.
Rate yourself again: How well do you handle setbacks and adversity? Compare to your Module 6 opening score.
